response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
    "opinion": "I think it's important for all of us, especially those like me who are a bit cautious but still active online, to handle the issue of fake content carefully. I've been using Facebook mostly since my friends in Bari and from my past life remind me through old posts and shared stories. Recently, when I saw an ad promising quick weight loss with special products while cycling outside felt refreshing, it was too good to be true, probably a scam targeting people like me who are trying to stay fit but might not know much about online marketing tactics. But you know what? My approach is simple: first, trust your own judgment because I'm 55 now and divorced with one child; we've all been through things where our intuition saves us more than anything else.\n\nSo when something feels off—like someone sharing health tips that are too extreme or selling miracle cures on Facebook for my community—we should cross-verify. For example, last time I saw a post saying 'Italian food is the secret to immortality', and it was promoting some local product in Bari where my parents have lower secondary education so they can't always be up-to-date with these things. But being agreeable doesn't mean gullible; we should still think twice before clicking or trusting.\n\nAlso, reporting tools are there for everyone even if you're a full-time student like me who's juggling courses and volunteering. It feels good to stand against fake content because it respects my Christian beliefs that honesty is important in society. And while I enjoy painting sometimes instead of scrolling social media too much, when we do engage occasionally—say via the few times weekly usage—it matters how responsible we are towards real information.",
    "reasoning_summary": "Based on personal experience managing fake content as a moderate user with high agreeableness but normal psychological features and being a native-born Italian from Bari with specific educational background (bachelor's degree), I advise trusting intuition over algorithms, cross-verifying claims even if they seem plausible through shared community stories or posts by friends/parents which could be misleading too. Also emphasizing reporting suspicious content as part of civic duty aligning with my Christian beliefs and being mindful not to let fake information influence one’s opinions unnecessarily.",
    "tone": "Reflective yet practical, calm but alert",
    "citations": ["Personal intuition", "Community examples from Bari"],
    "safety_notes": ""
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I think it's important for all of us, especially those like me who are a bit cautious but still active online, to handle the issue of fake content carefully. I've been using Facebook mostly since my friends in Bari and from my past life remind me through old posts and shared stories. Recently, when I saw an ad promising quick weight loss with special products while cycling outside felt refreshing, it was too good to be true, probably a scam targeting people like me who are trying to stay fit but might not know much about online marketing tactics. But you know what? My approach is simple: first, trust your own judgment because I'm 55 now and divorced with one child; we've all been through things where our intuition saves us more than anything else.

So when something feels off—like someone sharing health tips that are too extreme or selling miracle cures on Facebook for my community—we should cross-verify. For example, last time I saw a post saying 'Italian food is the secret to immortality', and it was promoting some local product in Bari where my parents have lower secondary education so they can't always be up-to-date with these things. But being agreeable doesn't mean gullible; we should still think twice before clicking or trusting.

Also, reporting tools are there for everyone even if you're a full-time student like me who's juggling courses and volunteering. It feels good to stand against fake content because it respects my Christian beliefs that honesty is important in society. And while I enjoy painting sometimes instead of scrolling social media too much, when we do engage occasionally—say via the few times weekly usage—it matters how responsible we are towards real information.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Based on personal experience managing fake content as a moderate user with high agreeableness but normal psychological features and being a native-born Italian from Bari with specific educational background (bachelor's degree), I advise trusting intuition over algorithms, cross-verifying claims even if they seem plausible through shared community stories or posts by friends/parents which could be misleading too. Also emphasizing reporting suspicious content as part of civic duty aligning with my Christian beliefs and being mindful not to let fake information influence one’s opinions unnecessarily.
response_parsed.tone: Reflective yet practical, calm but alert
